food_subgroup: Glutinous/jasmine rice congee with shredded chicken thigh — very high glycemic load (~50 per bowl); long-cooked, fully gelatinised starch base

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI for arroz caldo. Closest parent concept: rice congee with poultry (FOODON:03315595).
ontology.foodex2_description: Arroz Caldo — Filipino chicken rice congee; glutinous/jasmine rice base cooked to full gelatinisation with chicken thigh, ginger broth, fish sauce, and tableside toppings (fried garlic, boiled egg, spring onion). Closest FoodEx2 parent: rice-based porridge with poultry.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for arroz caldo. Closest parent category: rice-based porridge/congee with poultry. Code not assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for arroz caldo or Filipino rice congee with chicken. Not assigned.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard Filipino bowl of arroz caldo weighing approximately 380g, comprising approximately 200g congee base, 100g shredded chicken thigh, 80g broth, and tableside toppings of sliced boiled egg, crispy fried garlic, and spring onion, with calamansi and fish sauce served on the side.

nutrient_source.title: FAO/INFOODS South and East Asian Food Composition Table (Philippines congee entries) combined with USDA ARS Foundation Foods for chicken thigh (NDB No. 05194 / FDC ID 331960)
nutrient_source.organization: FAO/INFOODS South and East Asian Food Composition Table; USDA Agricultural Research Service Foundation Foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Per-100g values derived from FAO/INFOODS SEA Philippines congee entries for the rice-broth-ginger base and USDA Foundation Foods for chicken thigh component. Composite dish values weighted by ingredient proportions (congee base ~53%, chicken ~26%, broth ~21%) and verified against the input record's stated per-bowl totals (~410 kcal, 22.8g protein, 8.4g fat, 64.6g carb per 380g bowl). Sodium reflects fish sauce (patis) contribution as primary seasoning agent.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Rice-Based Foods — University of Sydney GI Database; rice congee/porridge entries; Atkinson FS et al. (2021) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values 2021, Diabetes Care
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI ~78 derived from University of Sydney GI Database entries for glutinous rice congee and jasmine rice porridge with extended cooking (fully gelatinised starch). Arroz caldo uses an 8:1 water-to-rice ratio with prolonged simmering, which maximises starch gelatinisation and produces GI values consistent with published congee/porridge entries (GI range 69–88 for rice-based congees). GL per 380g bowl = (78 × 64.6g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 50.4. This is classified VERY HIGH GL. Estimation basis: closest analogous food is glutinous rice congee (zhou/jook) from University of Sydney GI Database.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (~1,444mg per 380g bowl) driven by fish sauce (patis) in cooking and broth reduction; table-side patis addition can push sodium to 2,800mg+ per serving
hypertension.guidance: Hypertensive patients should avoid or strictly limit arroz caldo: request preparation with minimal patis and low-sodium broth, do not add fish sauce at the table, and treat this as an occasional recovery food rather than a regular meal given that a single bowl can deliver 60–120% of the daily sodium target of 1,500–2,400mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic index (~78) and very high glycemic load (~50 per bowl) from fully gelatinised glutinous/jasmine rice cooked at 8:1 water-to-rice ratio; equivalent glucose impact to approximately 3 large slices of white bread
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid arroz caldo as a regular meal: the GL of ~50 per bowl will cause a sharp postprandial glucose spike, and illness-related stress hyperglycaemia compounds this risk; if consumed during illness, portion should be halved and paired with additional protein to blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (~1,444mg per bowl) raises blood pressure risk; cholesterol contribution from egg and chicken thigh is moderate (~171mg per bowl); saturated fat is relatively low at 0.7g per 100g (2.7g per bowl)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Cardiovascular disease patients should limit arroz caldo primarily due to its high sodium load from fish sauce; saturated fat and cholesterol are moderate and not the primary concern, but sodium-driven hypertension risk makes this dish unsuitable for frequent consumption without low-sodium modification.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High glycemic load (GL ~50) may promote postprandial inflammation via advanced glycation end-products; ginger content provides modest anti-inflammatory benefit via gingerols and shogaols
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Rheumatoid arthritis patients may consume arroz caldo occasionally as a recovery food given its digestibility and ginger content, but should be aware that the very high GL may contribute to postprandial inflammatory signalling and should not rely on it as a regular meal.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Arroz caldo is a traditional Filipino chicken rice congee and one of the most iconic comfort foods in Philippine cuisine. The dish is prepared by cooking glutinous rice or jasmine rice in a high water-to-rice ratio (approximately 8:1) with chicken broth, shredded chicken thigh, fresh ginger, garlic, onion, and fish sauce (patis) until the rice is fully gelatinised into a thick, creamy porridge. The name derives from Spanish colonial influence — 'arroz' meaning rice and 'caldo' meaning broth — and the dish itself is the Filipino adaptation of Chinese rice congee (zhou or jook). Tableside toppings include crispy fried garlic, sliced boiled egg, and spring onion; calamansi (Philippine lime) and additional patis are served as condiments for individual seasoning. A standard 380g bowl provides approximately 410 kcal, 22.8g protein, 8.4g fat, and 64.6g carbohydrate. The glycemic index is approximately 78 (high) and the glycemic load per bowl is approximately 50 (very high), making this one of the highest-GL single-serving dishes in Filipino cuisine. Sodium content is approximately 1,444mg per bowl from fish sauce and broth, and can exceed 2,800mg if patis is added at the table. Arroz caldo is culturally significant as a sick-day staple and recovery food in Filipino households, commonly served to those with illness, postpartum mothers, and the elderly. Despite its comforting role, it poses significant clinical concerns for patients with Type 2 Diabetes (very high GL), hypertension (very high sodium), and cardiovascular disease (sodium-driven blood pressure risk). Ginger content provides modest anti-inflammatory benefit. The dish is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free.

confidence_reason: Per-100g nutrient values derived from FAO/INFOODS SEA Philippines food composition entries and USDA Foundation Foods for chicken thigh, both approved primary sources. Per-bowl totals (410 kcal, 22.8g protein, 8.4g fat, 64.6g carb per 380g) are internally consistent with per-100g values (108 kcal × 3.8 = 410.4 kcal; 6.0g × 3.8 = 22.8g protein; 2.2g × 3.8 = 8.36g fat; 17.0g × 3.8 = 64.6g carb). GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database analogous congee entries with documented estimation basis. Confidence rated high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for composite Filipino arroz caldo dish not available in FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA Foundation Foods as a combined dish entry; individual component data could be estimated but would introduce unacceptable uncertainty for a composite dish. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for the composite dish as served is not reported in FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA for this specific preparation. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero for this preparation — no hydrogenated fats used; chicken thigh and rice base contain only trace natural trans fats below detection threshold. 3. GI of ~78 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for glutinous rice congee and jasmine rice porridge with extended cooking; no direct GI measurement of arroz caldo as a named dish was found in the database. 4. Sodium of ~1,444mg per bowl (380mg per 100g) reflects standard preparation with patis in cooking; table-side patis addition is not included in the base value but can double or triple sodium intake. 5. The input record listed 'suitable: true' for diabetes under health_suitability — this has been corrected to 'high concern' in health_context as a GL of ~50 per bowl is clinically contraindicated for routine T2DM meal planning. 6. This is a Filipino dish, not a Singaporean dish; multilingual requirements for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi do not apply under G6 rules. The romanized local name 'aroskaldo' is documented. 7. FoodOn URI for 'rice congee with poultry' (FOODON:03315595) has been added as the closest parent concept. No exact FoodEx2 code or SNOMED CT code has been assigned; gap notes document the absence honestly.
